rawText: CAP published guidance on advertising hair loss products on 9 July 2026 which recognises three types of hair loss and states of chronic telogen effluvium (hair shedding): 'Importantly, neither the ASA nor CAP have seen evidence that products can treat this condition.' It adds that 'General claims about improving hair growth or thickness are often not permitted unless explicitly supported', that health claims may only be made if authorised on the Great Britain nutrition and health claims (NHC) register, that 'Testimonials do not replace clinical evidence', and that before-and-after images must reflect genuine, typical outcomes. CAP lists five recurring evidence failures: reliance on study abstracts or summaries rather than full clinical studies; studies not directly related to the product formulation; small unrepresentative sample sizes; lack of control groups or objective measurement; overreliance on subjective consumer surveys. · ANGLES: (1) Read the regulator's five evidence failures against the 'clinically proven' badges on hair supplements sold in the UK now. (2) Explain the three types of hair loss and which claims are permitted for each — a reusable buyer check. (3) The testimonial line — why a wall of before-and-afters is not evidence, in the regulator's own words. · APP CONNECTION: check evidence fit — paste a hair supplement's claim and the app checks whether it is an authorised NHC claim and whether the evidence matches the type of hair loss being claimed for. · SECONDARY: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/great-britain-nutrition-and-health-claims-nhc-register (GB NHC register, grade A); https://www.asa.org.uk/resource/health-beauty-and-slimming-claims-substantiation.html (CAP substantiation guidance, grade A) · SOURCE GRADE: A (regulator, fetched and verified 15 July 2026).
